title: French Sweet Crêpes

ingredients.name: maida (all-purpose flour)
visual_checks: about 120 g; sift to remove lumps
ingredients.notes: about 120 g; sift to remove lumps

subs.name: wheat flour (atta) for a slightly denser, more rustic crêpe — works well
subs.notes: the batter may need a splash more milk

subs.name: coconut milk for a dairy-free version with a subtle tropical note
subs.notes: thin it slightly with water if it seems too thick

ingredients.name: butter, melted and cooled
visual_checks: 1 tbsp in batter, 1 tbsp for greasing the pan
ingredients.notes: 1 tbsp in batter, 1 tbsp for greasing the pan

subs.name: coconut oil (1 tbsp) — keeps the crêpes light and dairy-free
a pinch of cardamom powder — adds an Indian warmth that pairs beautifully with banana and honey

ingredients.name: lemon, for squeezing at the table

steps.text: Whisk together the flour, eggs, sugar and pinch of salt in a bowl until a thick paste forms. Gradually pour in the milk a little at a time, whisking constantly to avoid lumps, then stir in 1 tablespoon of the melted butter and the vanilla. The batter should be thin — about the consistency of single cream. Cover and rest for at least 30 minutes at room temperature.
Heat a 20–22 cm non-stick frying pan or tawa over medium heat. Brush or dab lightly with the remaining melted butter using a folded piece of cloth or kitchen paper — you need only a thin film.

Pour a small ladleful (about 60–70 ml) of batter into the centre of the hot pan and immediately tilt and swirl the pan in a circular motion so the batter spreads into a thin, even round. Cook for about 1–1.5 minutes until the edges look dry and the underside is pale gold.

Flip the crêpe with a thin spatula — or with a confident wrist flick if you're feeling brave — and cook the second side for 30–40 seconds. The second side will look spottier and less even than the first, which is normal. Slide onto a plate and repeat with remaining batter.

Stack the crêpes as you go — they stay warm and soft this way. Serve with a squeeze of lemon and a dusting of sugar, a spoon of jam, fresh banana slices, or any topping you like. Crêpes can be made ahead and reheated briefly in a dry pan.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: maida → wheat flour (atta) for a slightly denser, more rustic crêpe — works well (the batter may need a splash more milk); milk → coconut milk for a dairy-free version with a subtle tropical note (thin it slightly with water if it seems too thick); butter → coconut oil (1 tbsp) — keeps the crêpes light and dairy-free (melt before using); vanilla → a pinch of cardamom powder — adds an Indian warmth that pairs beautifully with banana and honey (optional but recommended)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A flat non-stick tawa (dosa tawa) is ideal and makes perfect crêpes. The thin-batter technique is virtually the same as making a dosa — the main difference is the tawa should not be as hot as for dosa; keep it medium rather than high. Vanilla essence (not extract) is the most widely available flavouring in Indian stores and works well here.
